1.pericope_label: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
1.exposition: The gospel of free grace invites a sneer, and Paul lets it be heard: if grace superabounds where sin abounds (5:20), why not sin the more? His recoil is instant — by no means; how shall we who died to sin still live in it? The answer is not a rule but a fact: union with Christ. All who were baptized into Christ were baptized into his death, buried with him and raised to walk in newness of life. The old self was crucified and the body of sin broken (v.6); and as Christ died to sin once for all and now lives to God, so believers are to consider themselves dead to sin and alive to God. Only then does the imperative come: let not sin reign, present yourselves to God — for sin shall not have dominion over those who are under grace. The indicative grounds the imperative; what baptism enacted, faith is now to reckon and to obey.
1.original_language: Everything hangs on baptizō — to immerse, to plunge — and on the small preposition eis, into: we are baptized into Christ and into his death (v.3), the language of incorporation rather than mere ceremony. Synetaphēmen (we were buried with him, v.4) and symphytoi (grown together, grafted, v.5) make the union organic, while ephapax (once for all, v.10) fixes its ground in a single unrepeatable event. The hinge is the imperative logizesthe (v.11) — a present-tense keep reckoning, the very accounting verb used of the righteousness counted to Abraham in 4:3: not to pretend a fiction but to credit to your account what God has already made true.
1.historical_context: Baptism in the first century was no private rite but a public, community-witnessed boundary-crossing — the convert's decisive break with the old life, which is why Paul can appeal to it as something every reader has undergone and knows (v.3). The submerging and raising of the body naturally pictured burial and resurrection. Behind the whole passage stands a live controversy: Paul's law-free gospel was being slandered as an incitement to sin (3:8), and here he meets the charge head-on, turning from the doctrine of justification to the reality of sanctification.
1.christ: The section's engine is Christ's own history made ours. He died to sin once for all and now lives to God; his single, unrepeatable death exhausted sin's claim, and his resurrection is an endless life over which death no longer has dominion. To be buried and raised with him (v.4) is to share both movements — the death that breaks sin's rule and the risen life lived Godward. The believer's freedom is therefore not an achievement but a participation: what is true of the crucified and risen Lord is reckoned true of all who are united to him.

verses.note: Paul lets the objection speak in its boldest form: shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the perverse inference drawn from 5:20 — if grace multiplies where sin multiplies, why not sin the more? It is the very slander flung at Paul's law-free gospel (3:8). puritan Matthew Henry marks the turn the whole chapter now makes, from justification to sanctification, to show that grace does not license sin but destroys it.

By no means! How shall we who died to sin still live in it? grammatical-historical Robertson notes the vehement mē genoito, Paul's strongest recoil. reformed Calvin presses the ground: by union with Christ's death sin's dominion is already broken. So eastern Theodoret's point is simply the contradiction — for one who has died to sin to go on living in it is as absurd as a corpse resuming its old trade.

All of us baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death. grammatical-historical Robertson insists the sign proclaims a union it does not manufacture — Paul is no sacramentarian; the inward reality of being joined to Christ precedes the water. eastern Cyril and reformed Calvin add that baptism is nonetheless no empty token but the counterpart of Christ's sufferings, by which the old self is put to death.

We were buried with him by baptism into death, that we might walk in newness of life. grammatical-historical Clarke and wesleyan-arminian Wesley hear the immersion imagery — the body going under the water as into a grave. grammatical-historical Barnes cautions that Paul's aim is not to prescribe a mode but to teach death to sin. reformed Calvin fixes on the resurrection side: as Christ was raised by the Father's glory, the buried believer rises to a wholly new walk.

If we have been united with him in a death like his, we shall certainly be united with him in a resurrection like his. grammatical-historical Robertson unpacks symphytoi — grown together, grafted as a scion into its stock. eastern Chrysostom and reformed Calvin deny this is mere imitation of Christ's example: it is a real ingrafting by which his virtue passes into us, his death undoing our corruption, his rising the pledge that we too shall live.

Our old self was crucified with him, that the body of sin might be brought to nothing. grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke, with reformed Calvin, take the old man as the whole corrupt nature inherited from Adam (cf. Ephesians 4:22; Colossians 3:9), and the body of sin as the entire mass of corruption — not the material body — now rendered powerless. Barnes hears in the crucifixion a slow, painful subduing; Robertson adds that baptism pictures this death without effecting it, so that we should no longer be slaves of sin.

For one who has died has been set free from sin. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the axiom by analogy: as death cancels a master's claim upon his slave, so the one who has died with Christ is released from sin's legal and governing hold. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret call it no legal fiction but the deepest reality of the new life — the man once chained now stands beyond his old master's reach.

If we have died with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the foundation already laid in the preceding verses. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret draw the certainty: union with his death guarantees union with his life — a life both present, in newness of walk, and future, in resurrection glory.

Christ, being raised from the dead, will never die again; death no longer has dominion over him. grammatical-historical Barnes contrasts him with Lazarus, who rose only to die once more: Christ's resurrection life is endless. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret turn it toward us — because the Head lives beyond death's reach forever, the members joined to him hold a life that cannot finally be undone.

The death he died he died to sin once for all, but the life he lives he lives to God. grammatical-historical Robertson stresses the ephapax — a single, unrepeatable death that bore sin away for good. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret read Christ's pattern as the believer's own: dead to sin once, and henceforth wholly alive to God.

So you also must consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes note that reckon (logizesthe) is an accounting term — count it a settled fact, and apply Christ's once-for-all pattern (v.10) to yourself. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin call this the chapter's first command: no make-believe, but faith laying hold on the reality of union with Christ.

Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, to make you obey its passions. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the royal word reign — sin is a dethroned tyrant that must not be allowed to rule again. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that sin still remains and tempts through the body's appetites, but the believer is bound to refuse its bidding.

Do not present your members to sin as instruments for unrighteousness, but present yourselves to God. grammatical-historical Robertson observes that instruments (hopla) are weapons — the members are arms to be enlisted, and the only question is whose war they serve. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret hear the decisive consecration: the whole self, as those alive from the dead, handed over to God as weapons of righteousness.

Sin will have no dominion over you, since you are not under law but under grace. reformed Gill insists this is not a bare exhortation but an outright promise: sin's tyranny is broken. grammatical-historical Barnes and wesleyan-arminian Wesley, with Calvin, explain the contrast — under law is the covenant of works, which exposes sin but gives no power against it; under grace is the reign of mercy that both pardons and supplies the Spirit's strength. eastern Theodoret sums it up: grace lends the help that the law only demanded.

witnesses.point: Paul never sets doctrine apart from exhortation: the very fact that we were grafted into Christ's death is itself the summons to walk in newness of life.

The vehement 'God forbid' (mē genoito) rejects the antinomian inference; baptism proclaims a union with Christ it does not create, and 'reckon' (logizesthe) bids us count as settled fact what is already true.
Union with Christ is no bare imitation but a real ingrafting — his death destroying our corruption, his risen life renewing us — so that to live on in sin flatly contradicts our baptism.
The first command of the chapter is to reckon ourselves dead to sin and alive to God; from this faith laying hold on our union all resistance to sin proceeds.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: To have 'died to sin' is to have been transferred out of the old realm where sin reigns as lord into the new realm of grace — a decisive change of dominion and belonging, not yet a sinless perfection, which is why the imperative to resist has real traction.

1.application: Sanctification here does not begin with gritted teeth but with a reckoning — counting true what God has already declared, that you have died to sin and are alive to God in Christ. Yet the reckoning is not passive: having counted it so, you are to refuse sin the throne and hand your very body over to God as a weapon in his hands. Believe what is true, then live it.

15.exposition: The objection returns in a new key: if we are not under law but under grace, may we then sin? Again Paul recoils, and this time answers from the world of the slave market. You are the property of whatever you obey — sin, whose wage is death, or obedience, whose issue is righteousness (v.16); there is no neutral ground. He thanks God the Romans, once slaves of sin, have obeyed from the heart the pattern of teaching handed to them, and so, freed from sin, have become slaves of righteousness. Apologizing for the harsh figure (v.19), he weighs the two slaveries by their harvest: sin yields shame now and death at the last (v.21), while the service of God yields holiness now and eternal life at the end. Then the chapter is crowned in a single sentence of the whole gospel — the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
15.original_language: Paul's controlling image is doulos — not a hired servant but a slave, owned in body and will (v.16); the point is that no one is his own master. In v.17 the gospel is a tupos didachēs, a pattern or mold of teaching into which the convert is poured and shaped. The last verse turns on a deliberate mismatch of nouns: opsōnia (wages — a soldier's pay or rations, what is strictly owed) set against charisma (a free gift, a gift of grace). Sin pays out what it owes; God gives what is not owed — the whole gospel folded into one antithesis (v.23).
15.historical_context: Slavery saturated the Roman world; a large share of the empire's population was owned, and the absolute claim of a master on his slave's obedience was a fact every reader knew in the bone. Paul exploits that familiarity — and feels its harshness, apologizing that he speaks in human terms (v.19). The wage-word opsōnia likewise came from ordinary life: the ration or stipend paid to a soldier, earned and reckoned as due — which makes the swerve to free gift in v.23 land with such force.
15.christ: The section ends where the whole argument has been tending: eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. This is no generic immortality but a share in the risen Christ's own life — the same being alive to God in Christ Jesus of v.11. The gift, in the end, is the person: to receive eternal life is to be joined to the one who conquered death. And the closing title Lord (kyrios) names the risen Christ whose lordship is the true alternative to sin's dominion — a new master who liberates rather than enslaves, and gives rather than demands.

verses.note: Are we to sin because we are not under law but under grace? By no means! grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson see the antinomian objection return in a new dress, now feeding on the promise of v.14, and again meeting Paul's flat mē genoito. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the answer already forming: grace does not loose us to sin but binds us as servants of righteousness.

You are slaves of the one whom you obey — either of sin, which leads to death, or of obedience, which leads to righteousness. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson state the axiom: obedience settles the question of ownership, and there is no third, masterless option. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin apply it — the one who obeys God under grace is God's slave, not sin's, so grace can license no sin.

Thanks be to God that you who were slaves of sin have become obedient from the heart. grammatical-historical Robertson and reformed Calvin dwell on the standard of teaching to which they were committed — tupos, a mold or pattern into which the believers were poured and shaped by the gospel. The obedience is from the heart, inward and genuine, and the thanks go to God who wrought the change.

Having been set free from sin, you became slaves of righteousness. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress that this freedom is not autonomy but an exchange of masters. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret name the paradox at the heart of the gospel: true liberty is the glad service of righteousness, the tyranny of sin traded for the blessed lordship of God.

I am speaking in human terms, because of your natural limitations. eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical Barnes note the apology: Paul borrows the harsh figure of slavery to make the truth vivid to weak understanding. As once they yielded their members to impurity and to lawlessness leading to more lawlessness, so now they are to yield them to righteousness — and reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret mark the goal, unto holiness, a real growth in sanctification.

When you were slaves of sin, you were free in regard to righteousness. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson catch the bitter irony — the only freedom sin grants is a freedom from righteousness, no liberty at all but the deepest bondage. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that Paul recalls that old state on purpose, to shame the former service and set off the blessedness of the new.

What fruit were you getting from the things of which you are now ashamed? The end of those things is death. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson answer for the conscience: no profit worth having, only the shame the awakened heart now feels. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin fix on the terminus — those things end not merely in regret but in death.

But now, set free from sin and become slaves of God, the fruit you get leads to sanctification, and its end, eternal life. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the happy reversal of the old bondage. reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril weigh the two harvests by their ends: sin's service closes in death, God's service in everlasting life.

For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson press opsōnia — a soldier's pay: death is the just, earned wage that sin has coming. grammatical-historical Ellicott and wesleyan-arminian Clarke mark the deliberate swerve of the antithesis — not wages now but charisma, a free gift: eternal life cannot be earned. reformed Gill adds that even the gift comes only through Christ, for a man may earn hell but can never merit heaven.

witnesses.point: One is the slave of whatever he obeys; the readers were poured into the 'mold' (tupos) of gospel teaching, and 'wages' (opsōnia) in v.23 is the soldier's pay — death is what sin justly owes.
Christian freedom is not autonomy but a change of masters: freed from sin, we are bound to righteousness, and this glad servitude to God is the only true liberty.
Paul apologizes for the lowly figure of slavery, speaking 'after the manner of men' because of our weakness, to press home how total our devotion to righteousness must be.
Since obedience settles the question of mastery, the one who obeys God is God's servant and not sin's; grace therefore gives no license to sin but binds us the more closely to holiness.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: The slavery-and-freedom argument retells the exodus: as Israel was redeemed from bondage in Egypt to serve the Lord, so believers are brought out of slavery to sin, through the waters, into the glad service of a new master whose end is not death but life.

15.application: There is no masterless life on offer; every person serves something, and the only question is which lord, and how he pays. Sin pays wages you have truly earned — it always settles its accounts, and it settles them in death. God gives a gift you could never earn, and the gift is his own Son. Freedom, then, is not escaping all service but being owned by the Master whose service is life.

_meta.title: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
reflection: Paul commands believers to consider themselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus (v.11). What is the difference between reckoning as true what God has already accomplished and simply trying harder to stop sinning?

Paul urges you to present yourself to God as one brought from death to life, and your members to God as instruments for righteousness (v.13). To which master are your daily members — hands, words, attention — actually being handed over this week?
The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord (v.23). Where are you still trying to earn as a wage what God is offering only as a gift?
